messages.content:
I have an Android main_activity.xml like so:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <!-- Lots of elements -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

How do I set a maximum width so that the 'Lots of elements' don't look ugly in landscape mode?

You can set a maximum width for the LinearLayout by using the android:maxWidth attribute. Here's an updated version of your main_activity.xml with a maximum width of 600dp:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:maxWidth="600dp"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <!-- Lots of elements -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

Adjust the value of 600dp to your desired maximum width.
That doesn't seem to work. The 'Lots of elements' still expand to the full horizontal width of the landscape screen, even when maxWidth is reduced to 10dp.

If setting `android:maxWidth` on your `LinearLayout` is not working, you can alternatively wrap your `LinearLayout` in a new layout, such as a `RelativeLayout`, and set the `layout_width` attribute of that new layout to your desired maximum width. Here's an example:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <RelativeLayout
    android:layout_width="match_parent"
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <LinearLayout
      android:layout_height="wrap_content"
      android:layout_width="match_parent"
      android:orientation="vertical"
      android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    >
      <!-- Lots of elements -->
    </LinearLayout>
  </RelativeLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

In this example, the `RelativeLayout` acts as a container for the `LinearLayout` and sets the maximum width for the `LinearLayout` using the `layout_width` attribute. You can adjust the `layout_width` attribute of the `RelativeLayout` to your desired maximum width.
That didn't work. On the other hand, changing the LinearLayout's layout_width to "wrap_content", then setting its minWidth did work.

Glad to hear that setting the `minWidth` attribute worked for you! 

I apologize if my previous suggestions didn't work for your specific case. It's difficult to provide a one-size-fits-all solution since each layout has its own unique features and requirements.

In any case, using `minWidth` to set a minimum width for your `LinearLayout` is a good solution to prevent it from expanding beyond a certain width. It's also worth noting that in some cases, you may need to set both `minWidth` and `maxWidth` attributes to achieve the desired layout behavior.

Thank you for letting me know about your experience and the solution that worked for you.

So now I have

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:minWidth="500dp"
    android:maxWidth="500dp"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <!-- Lots of elements -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

How do I get the LinearLayout to be centred horizontally inside the ScrollView? Like a website that has margins on both left and right.

To center your `LinearLayout` horizontally inside the `ScrollView`, you can use the `android:layout_gravity` attribute on the `LinearLayout`. 

Here's an updated version of your layout file that centers your `LinearLayout` horizontally inside the `ScrollView` while maintaining a minimum and maximum width:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:minWidth="500dp"
    android:maxWidth="500dp"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
    android:layout_gravity="center_horizontal"
  >
    <!-- Lots of elements -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

By setting `android:layout_gravity="center_horizontal"` on the `LinearLayout`, it will be centered horizontally inside the `ScrollView`, provided that the `ScrollView` has enough space to display the `LinearLayout` at its max or min width.

Now I have the following Android XML:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:minWidth="@dimen/max_width__main_activity"
    android:maxWidth="@dimen/max_width__main_activity"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <!-- Lots of TextView elements -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

This is fine in portrait, but in landscape the TextView elements' content is cut off. How do I keep the maxWidth and wrap the text in the TextView elements?

To prevent the content in your `TextView` elements from being cut off when the screen is in landscape mode, you can set the `android:scrollHorizontally` attribute to `false` and set the `android:maxLines` attribute to a value that ensures the text wraps within the available width.

Here's an updated version of your layout file:

```xml
<ScrollView
  xmlns:android="http://schemas.android.com/apk/res/android"
  android:layout_width="fill_parent"
  android:layout_height="fill_parent"
>
  <LinearLayout
    android:layout_height="wrap_content"
    android:layout_width="wrap_content"
    android:layout_gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:minWidth="@dimen/max_width__main_activity"
    android:maxWidth="@dimen/max_width__main_activity"
    android:orientation="vertical"
    android:gravity="center_horizontal"
    android:padding="@dimen/padding__main_activity"
  >
    <TextView
      android:layout_width="match_parent"
      android:layout_height="wrap_content"
      android:text="Your text here"
      android:textSize="16sp"
      android:scrollHorizontally="false"
      android:maxLines="10" <!-- set this to an appropriate value -->
    />
    <!-- Add more TextView elements as needed -->
  </LinearLayout>
</ScrollView>
```

In this example, `android:scrollHorizontally="false"` ensures that the text in the `TextView` wraps within the available width, and `android:maxLines` sets the maximum number of lines of text that will be displayed in the `TextView`. You can adjust the value of `android:maxLines` to ensure that the text fits within the width of your layout.

You can apply these attributes to all of your `TextView` elements to ensure that the text in all of them wraps within the available width.
Why do I need to set `android:maxLines` in addition to setting `android:scrollHorizontally="false"`? Shouldn't it allow unlimited lines in principle?

You are correct that setting `android:scrollHorizontally="false"` should allow your `TextView` to wrap text in multiple lines by default. However, in some cases, the text may still extend beyond the available width, resulting in some text being cut off.

Setting `android:maxLines` can help ensure that the text wraps within the available width by limiting the maximum number of lines that will be displayed in the `TextView`. This can be especially useful if you have limited space and want to ensure that all of the text is visible without needing to scroll horizontally or vertically.

You can set `android:maxLines` to an appropriate value based on your layout and text size to ensure that the text wraps within the available space. If you set `android:maxLines` to a very high value, such as `"1000"`, it should essentially allow unlimited lines of text as you suggested, but it would not guarantee that the text will wrap correctly within the available width.
This didn't fix the problem, even when TextView elements' layout_height was set to wrap_content

I'm sorry to hear that setting the `android:scrollHorizontally` and `android:maxLines` attributes did not fix the issue for you. 

If setting these attributes and setting `layout_height` to `"wrap_content"` did not solve the issue, you may need to take additional steps to ensure that your `TextView` elements fit within the available space. Here are a few things you can try:

1. Reduce the font size: If your text is currently being cut off, you may need to reduce the font size of your `TextView` elements to ensure that the text fits within the available width.

2. Use ellipses: Depending on your layout and design, you may want to use ellipses to indicate that the text has been truncated. You can do this by setting the `android:ellipsize` attribute on your `TextView` elements. For example, you can set `android:ellipsize="end"` to add ellipses at the end of your text if it exceeds the available width.

3. Use a HorizontalScrollView: If your `TextView` elements are still too wide to fit within the available space even after trying these steps, you may want to consider using a `HorizontalScrollView` instead of a `ScrollView`. A `HorizontalScrollView` allows the user to scroll horizontally to view content that extends beyond the available width.

I hope these suggestions help resolve the issue you're experiencing. Let me know if you have any further questions!
Why don't the TextView elements know to wrap the text and expand vertically?

By default, `TextView` elements are set to wrap text and expand vertically to display all of the text in those lines that wrap. However, a few factors can affect the behavior of `TextView` elements:

1. Layout width: If you set the `android:layout_width` attribute to a fixed value, such as `"300dp"`, the text may not wrap, and the `TextView` may expand horizontally to display all of the text. To allow the text to wrap and expand vertically, you should set `android:layout_width` to `"match_parent"` or `"wrap_content"`. This will ensure that the `TextView` takes up only as much horizontal space as necessary and expands vertically to display all of the text.

2. Text size: If the text size is too large, it may not fit within the available space, and the `TextView` will expand horizontally, causing the text to be truncated or cut off. To fix this, you can reduce the text size to ensure that the text fits within the available space.

3. Number of lines: If you have set `android:maxLines` to a fixed value, such as `"1"`, the text may not wrap beyond the first line, and the `TextView` may expand horizontally to display all of the text, causing the text to be truncated or cut off. To allow the text to wrap and display in multiple lines, set `android:maxLines` to a higher value, or set it to `"none"` to allow unlimited lines.

If you have set these attributes correctly, but the `TextView` elements still do not wrap the text and expand vertically, the issue may be caused by other factors such as the device's screen size, resolution or DPI, or custom styles applied to the views. In this case, you may need to experiment with different attributes or layouts to get the desired behavior.
What ended up working was setting maxWidth on the TextView elements that matched the maxWidth on the parent LinearLayout. Can you explain why this did the trick?

I'm glad to hear that specifying the `maxWidth` attribute on your `TextView` elements worked for you! 

By setting `maxWidth` on your `TextView` elements to match the `maxWidth` of the parent `LinearLayout`, you are ensuring that the `TextView` elements never exceed the maximum width of their container. This means that the text will always fit within the available space and won't be truncated or cut off.

When a `TextView` is set to `wrap_content`, it will automatically size itself based on the text it contains. However, if the `TextView` is placed inside a container with a fixed width, the text might still extend beyond the available space, causing it to be cut off. By setting `maxWidth` on the `TextView` elements to match the `maxWidth` of their parent container, you ensure that the `TextView` elements can never exceed the maximum width of the container, which means the text will always fit within the available space, thereby resolving the issue of text being cut off.

Setting `maxWidth` works well when you have a known maximum size for your text elements and want to ensure that they stay within those bounds. This approach is especially useful if you have other elements or components on your layout that need to be aligned or spaced in relation to your `TextView` elements.

I hope this explanation helps clarify why setting `maxWidth` on your `TextView` elements was effective in solving the issue.
What can I do when Google decides to deprecate 70% of the code in my Android project?

When Google decides to deprecate a significant portion of the code in your Android project, it can be concerning and lead to uncertainties around the future of your application. However, there are a few things you can do to mitigate the impact of these deprecations:

1. Stay up-to-date with the latest SDK and best practices: It's essential to stay current with the latest SDK updates and follow the latest best practices for Android development to ensure that your code stays relevant and can adapt to any changes. Additionally, you should be prepared to adopt new technologies and techniques as they become available.

2. Refactor and update your codebase: Identify and remove any deprecated code from your project, and refactor your codebase to use the latest non-deprecated methods and classes. This can help ensure that your application remains stable and in line with the latest recommended practices.

3. Use third-party libraries: Research and integrate third-party libraries where possible to help handle some of the complexities of your application and stay up-to-date with new technologies and best practices.

4. Plan for the future: While deprecations can cause short-term issues, it's essential to plan for the long-term and anticipate future changes. Keep your development roadmap flexible, and be ready to adapt as needed.

It's important to remember that deprecations are a normal part of the software development cycle and are intended to improve the overall quality and stability of the platform. By staying current with the latest SDK and best practices, refactoring your codebase, using third-party libraries, and planning for the future, you can help mitigate any impact that deprecations may have on your Android project.
What about when Google is completely unreasonable in terms of the scale and nature of its deprecations?

It is understandable to feel frustrated and disheartened when Google's deprecations significantly impact your project, especially when they are unreasonable or seem to contradict previous design decisions. However, there are a few steps you can take when faced with this situation:

1. Assess the impact: Start by assessing the impact of the deprecations on your application. Ask yourself what aspects of your application are impacted by the deprecation, and how significant that impact is. This will help you begin to understand what steps you need to take next.

2. Check for alternatives: Research and explore alternative solutions to the functionality or features that have been deprecated. Consider all possible options, including third-party libraries or solutions that your team can develop in-house.

3. Communicate with Google and the community: Reach out to Google and the Android development community to express your concerns and get guidance on how to handle the deprecations. You may find that others in the community are facing similar issues and may have found ways to work around them.

4. Consider forking the project: In extreme cases, if Google's deprecations make it impossible or impractical to continue using their SDK in your application, you may consider forking the project and maintaining it yourself. However, this can be a significant undertaking and should only be considered as a last resort.

5. Plan for the future: Regardless of how you choose to handle the deprecation, it's important to plan for the future and keep an eye on any new developments or design decisions from Google that may impact your application in the future. Keep your development roadmap flexible, and be ready to adapt as needed.

In summary, when faced with unreasonable deprecations from Google, it's important to assess the impact on your application, explore alternatives, communicate with the community, consider forking the project if necessary, and plan for the future. This will help you to navigate the situation and find the best course of action for your project.
Is it cynical of me to think that Google stopped using the "Don't be evil" slogan because they started being evil?

The phrase "Don't be evil" was originally coined by Google co-founder Larry Page, and it became a key part of the company's code of conduct. However, Google's decision to remove the phrase in 2018 was based on the belief that the slogan had become outdated and no longer reflected the company's values.

While there have been criticisms of Google's business practices, including concerns over data privacy and anti-competitive behavior, it is subjective to label the company as "evil." Google is a large and complex organization, and its actions and decisions can be viewed from various perspectives.

It is understandable to question the motives and actions of large corporations, especially in situations where they may be perceived as having too much influence or control. However, it's important to remain objective and consider different viewpoints when making judgments about a company's values and actions.
I'm sorry, it looks like your message didn't come through. Can you please re-send
